/* CALLOUT (split disclosure) */ .callout-box{background:rgba(200,146,26,.1);border:1px solid rgba(200,146,26,.4);border-radius:10px;padding:24px 28px;margin:32px 0;font-family:'Inter',sans-serif} .callout-box p{font-size:15px;color:var(--light);margin:0 0 12px;line-height:1.7} .callout-box p:last-child{margin-bottom:0} .callout-box strong{color:var(--gold2)} .screenshot-btn{display:inline-block;margin-left:10px;padding:3px 10px;background:rgba(200,146,26,.15);border:1px solid rgba(200,146,26,.4);border-radius:5px;color:var(--gold2);font-size:12px;font-weight:600;text-decoration:none;font-family:'Inter',sans-serif} .citation-list{font-family:'Inter',sans-serif;font-size:14px;color:var(--light);line-height:2.2} .citation-list a{color:var(--light)} /* MODAL */ .modal-overlay{display:none;position:fixed;top:0;left:0;right:0;bottom:0;background:rgba(5,10,18,.85);z-index:500;align-items:center;justify-content:center;padding:24px} .modal-box{background:var(--navy2);border:1px solid rgba(200,146,26,.3);border-radius:14px;max-width:700px;width:100%;padding:28px;position:relative;max-height:88vh;overflow-y:auto} .modal-close{position:absolute;top:16px;right:18px;background:none;border:none;color:var(--gray);font-size:24px;cursor:pointer;line-height:1} .modal-close:hover{color:var(--gold2)} .modal-box h4{font-family:'Georgia',serif;color:#fff;font-size:19px;margin:0 0 6px} .modal-box .modal-sub{font-family:'Inter',sans-serif;color:var(--gray);font-size:13px;margin:0 0 18px}

Home› Blog› How a P&F Chart Predicted Apple's Bull Run
Case Study · AAPL

How a Point & Figure Chart Predicted Apple's Greatest Bull Run Before Wall Street Saw It

By Karim Pirani July 5, 2026 7 min read
This Article at a Glance

On July 24, 2013, AAPL gapped up and printed $443.45, piercing its Bearish Resistance Line at $440 — a Double Top Breakout with a preliminary bullish price objective of $496. The Golden Cross followed almost exactly on schedule. Apple has split twice since (7-for-1 in 2014, 4-for-1 in 2020, a combined 28x), so every price below is shown in both its original 2013 terms and today's split-adjusted terms.

Apple in the summer of 2013 was not a popular stock. It had fallen from its September 2012 high near $705 to the $385–$440 range, a decline steep enough that financial media had settled into a familiar refrain: Apple's growth story was over, margins were compressing, and the next great idea wasn't coming.

None of that mattered to a point and figure chart. Point & Figure — sometimes written without the ampersand — is one of the oldest forms of technical analysis still in active use, and it doesn't care about sentiment, headlines, or analyst notes. It cares about one thing: whether demand or supply is currently winning.

On July 26, 2013, I published the first of a three-part series laying out a specific, falsifiable call: Apple had just pierced its Bearish Resistance Line, and the chart was pointing to a move well past $700.

The Signal: A BRL Pierce

Every Point & Figure chart carries a Bearish Resistance Line (BRL) — a 45-degree line that caps every rally attempt during a downtrend. As long as price stays under it, sellers are still in control. The moment a column of X's breaks through it, the entire structure of the decline is broken. That's the pierce — one of the clearest buy signals in the P&F toolkit, and in 25+ years of trading it, the single most reliable one I've found.

That's exactly what AAPL did the week of July 24, 2013.

Original Chart · StockCharts.com, July 24, 2013

The actual chart from that day — unedited, timestamped, straight off StockCharts.com. AAPL gapped up and printed $443.45, breaking through the BRL sitting at $440. Preliminary bullish price objective: $496.

Two Splits Since — Here's the Same Chart, Today's Terms

⚠ A note on price, for anyone checking a modern chart: every price above is in 2013 terms, exactly as published at the time. Apple has split twice since — 7-for-1 in June 2014, then 4-for-1 in August 2020 — a combined 28x adjustment. On today's split-adjusted chart, the $440 BRL level works out to roughly $15.71, the $443.45 breakout print to roughly $15.84, and the $496 price objective to roughly $17.71.

See this same call plotted on today's split-adjusted P&F chart →

The Golden Cross arrived within the predicted window — 12 to 15 trading days, exactly as called in Part 2 of the series. From the low $440s, Apple went on to trade above $700 (pre-split) within the following year.

“The goal is not to be right every day. The goal is to be right when it matters — when the stock is actually ready to move. Point & Figure tells you when that is.”

— Karim Pirani, Founder & Architect, OpusEngine™

Read the Original Posts

None of this is reconstructed after the fact. All three parts of the original series are still live on Seeking Alpha, exactly as published in 2013:

Part 1 — The Impending Move In Apple (Jul 26, 2013) View Screenshot ↗
Part 2 — The Impending Move In Apple (Aug 19, 2013) View Screenshot ↗
Part 3 — The Impending Move In Apple (Aug 28, 2013) View Screenshot ↗

Why the Chart Saw It First

None of this required knowing what Tim Cook was planning or what margins would do next quarter. A BRL pierce is a statement about supply and demand: sellers, who had controlled every rally for months, had just failed to turn back an advance for the first time. That's visible on a P&F chart the moment it happens — well before it shows up in a headline or an analyst note.

Fundamentals tell you what a company is worth. Price structure tells you when the market is about to agree with you. OpusEngine™ is built to surface exactly this kind of structural trading signal — systematically, across a full watchlist, generating stock signals instead of one chart at a time.

Key Takeaways
  • AAPL pierced its Bearish Resistance Line at $440 on July 24, 2013, printing $443.45
  • The Double Top Breakout carried a preliminary price objective of $496
  • Golden Cross followed 12–15 trading days later, exactly as called
  • Apple ran past $700 (pre-split) within the following year
  • Two splits since (7-for-1 in 2014, 4-for-1 in 2020) mean a combined 28x price adjustment
  • All three original posts remain live on Seeking Alpha, timestamped in 2013

Same BRL pierce, same $496 preliminary price objective — rescaled for both splits, a combined 28x adjustment. No split-adjusted version of this chart existed in 2013, so this is a reconstruction rather than a screenshot.
